title: Chicken fajitas
subtitle: Marinated thigh strips, peppers, soft tortillas at the table.
excerpt: Strips of marinated chicken thigh seared fast in a screaming-hot pan with sliced peppers and onion, brought to the table sizzling. Warm tortillas, soured cream, salsa, and a wedge of lime alongside. Build to taste.
sourceNotes: The fajita technique developed from the Mexican-American ranch kitchens of South Texas in the early twentieth century, where skirt steak (the original cut) was marinated and grilled. The chicken version came later as a popular substitute.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The marinade can be mixed and the chicken added up to 8 hours ahead. Don't marinate longer than overnight; the lime juice will start to cook the chicken.

content.text: Boneless chicken thigh cut into 2 cm strips, marinated for an hour in lime juice, oil, garlic, and dried spice. The pan goes on the highest heat until it smokes faintly. Chicken in first, in a single layer, left alone for 3 minutes to catch colour. Pulled out into a warm dish. Peppers and onion go in next, charred fast, then the chicken returns for 2 more minutes to bind everything together.
Thigh meat over breast. Thighs stay juicy at the speed this dish demands; breast turns to chalk.

items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, cut into 2 cm strips
two for the marinade, one cut in wedges for serving

content.text: A cast-iron pan is the right tool: it holds the high heat and gives the proper char. Anything thinner will lose temperature when the chicken hits.

Squeeze the juice of 2 of the limes into a large bowl. Whisk in {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{garlic}} of chopped garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{oregano-dried}} of oregano, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes if using, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Add the {{chicken-thigh}} of chicken strips and turn to coat. Cover and refrigerate for 60 minutes.

Heat the cast-iron pan over the highest setting until you can see a faint haze. Lift the chicken from the marinade with tongs, letting excess drip off. Spread in a single layer in the pan; don't crowd. Cook 3 minutes without moving, until the underside is dark gold. Turn and cook 2 minutes more. Tip into a warm serving dish, cover loosely.

Wipe the pan with kitchen paper. Return to high heat. Add {{vegetable-oil}} of oil and tip in {{pepper-red}} of red pepper, {{pepper-green}} of green pepper, and {{onion}} of sliced onion. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring once or twice, until charred at the edges and still crisp in the middle. Tip back into the dish with the chicken and toss together.
Stack the tortillas, wrap in a clean tea towel, and microwave for 30 seconds; or warm in a dry frying pan, 15 seconds a side. Wrap in foil or a tea towel to hold the warmth.
Bring everything to the table at once: the sizzling chicken-and-pepper dish, the warm tortillas, the soured cream, the wedges of lime, and the chopped coriander. Build each taco at the table.

items.cause: Pan too crowded, or temperature too low.
items.fix: Cook in two batches if the pan is small. The chicken should hiss audibly the moment it hits the pan.

items.cause: Cooked too long, or sliced too thin.
items.fix: Slice peppers 1 cm thick. Five minutes total in the hot pan is enough; they should still have bite.

items.cause: Breast used instead of thigh, or overcooked.
items.fix: Use boneless thigh; it stays juicy at high heat. Pull off the pan at 5 minutes total.

items.cause: Tortillas too cold, or filled too generously.
items.fix: Warm the tortillas just before serving and wrap in a tea towel. Two tablespoons of filling per tortilla, not more.

content.text: Fajitas were Mexican ranch food in the Rio Grande Valley, where the throwaway skirt steak from the day's beef butcher was tossed with onion and grilled over wood. They reached the national American palate in the 1980s through restaurants in San Antonio and Austin. Drink a margarita with salt on the rim, or a cold Mexican lager.
